figures.caption: Figure 1 Paradigms for grounding driving policies in 3D geometry. Existing approaches expose 3D structure in different ways, but each misses one key capability. (a) VLAs with sparse 3D perception use structured 3D supervision such as boxes, occupancy, and lane maps, but lack dense spatial grounding. (b) Injection-based VLAs expose dense 3D features to the language model, but lack dedicated geometry capacity. (c) Geometry-only driving policies provide dense spatial grounding with dedicated geometry capacity, but remove language reasoning. (d) VLGA preserves all three by introducing a parameter-isolated geometry expert supervised with dense geometry reconstruction.
Table 3 Per-skill closed-loop success rate (%) on Bench2Drive.

Table 2 Closed-loop driving on Bench2Drive in CARLA. The leading Avg. L2 column reports open-loop 3 s trajectory error, where lower is better; all other columns are official closed-loop metrics, where higher is better.

Figure 4 Pointmap reconstructions with predicted and ground-truth trajectories on nuScenes. Each row is one validation sample. Left: the six surround-camera inputs. Right: the dense 3D pointmap predicted by VLGA’s geometry expert (decoded by D for visualization), with the green ground-truth and yellow predicted ego trajectories overlaid.

Figure 2 VLGA architecture. A four-expert Mixture-of-Transformers coupled by masked joint attention: an understanding expert (U , language/scene semantics), a perception expert (P , sparse agent/map/occupancy queries), our new geometry expert (G, dense spatial structure), and an action expert (A, motion planning). A attends to U , P , G and conditions on ego status to emit the trajectory.
Table 4 Ablation on VLGA’s geometric stream components, on nuScenes open-loop planning (ST-P3 metric, without ego status). Each row progressively adds one component to the baseline.
